content:
# Drilling Time Optimization Using differential evolution

**Summary**: The thesis investigates the optimization of drilling time and bit life in two sections (12.25" and 8.5") using a Differential Evolution algorithm. By optimizing parameters like Weight on Bit (WOB), rotation speed, and flow rate, the study demonstrates significant reductions in total drilling time and bit counts compared to actual field cases.

---

## Research Problem

The inefficiency of manual selection of drilling parameters (WOB, RPM, flow rate) leads to suboptimal Rates of Penetration (ROP) and increased drilling time. The study addresses how to use a mathematical model (Warren's model) combined with an evolutionary algorithm (Differential Evolution) to optimize these parameters and predict bit wear accurately.

## Objectives

- Use Differential Evolution as an optimization technique to minimize drilling time and total time required to drill a section.
- Identify optimal parameters (WOB, RPM, flow rate) that balance high ROP with extended bit life.
- Demonstrate that maximizing ROP does not always result in the minimum total drilling time.
- Determine bit coefficients (a, b, c) and wear coefficients (Wc) from field data for the 12.25" and 8.5" sections.
- Validate Warren's ROP model against actual drilling data in both well sections.
- Optimize drilling parameters (Flow rate, Rotation speed, Weight on bit) using the Differential Evolution algorithm.

## Methods

The study utilizes Warren's model to calculate ROP based on bit coefficients and wear. Bit coefficients are determined by plotting $(ND/F(pe) 	imes R)$ vs $(S^2 D^4 / W^2)$ for the first 100m of a run. The bit wear coefficient (Wc) is calculated via iteration to determine the wear factor (Wf). A Differential Evolution (DE) algorithm, implemented in MATLAB, is used as the optimization engine. DE parameters include population size (Np), mutation factor (F), and crossover factor (CR). The objective function for the DE is the total drilling time, which includes both drilling time and a fixed allowance for bit changes.

## Data and Materials

Data from Well A1 in Saudi Arabia; 30 intervals of 50m each for the 12.25" section (700m to 2200m) and 20 intervals of 50m each for the 8.5" section (2200m to 3200m). Parameters include bit sizes (24", 17.5", 12.25", 8.5"), mud properties, and rock strength (UCS) values ranging from 8000 to 18000 psi.

## Key Findings

- The study identified that maximizing ROP (e.g., by increasing WOB or RPM) can lead to accelerated bit wear, potentially negating time gains from faster drilling due to frequent bit changes. ([[2014-ahmed|Ahmed, 2014, p. 21]])
- A modified Warren's model was selected as the most representative for actual conditions because it accounts for bit wear and rock strength (S). ([[2014-ahmed|Ahmed, 2014, p. 32]])
- The average ROP from MLD and DDR data provided a more accurate representation of drilling performance than either source alone. ([[2014-ahmed|Ahmed, 2014, p. 46]])
- Differential Evolution (DE) was shown to be an effective stochastic search algorithm for locating global optimum parameters in complex, non-linear drilling models. ([[2014-ahmed|Ahmed, 2014, p. 20]])
- The average estimation of the trips for each bit run was determined to be approximately 5 hrs/1000 ft, plus a 2-hour allowance for surface bit changes. ([[2014-ahmed|Ahmed, 2014, p. 55]])
- In the 12.25" section, the optimization resulted in a reduction of drilling time from 251 hours (actual) to 201 hours (optimized), representing a 21% reduction. ([[2014-ahmed|Ahmed, 2014, p. 68]])
- The average UCS for the AG formation in Well A1 was found to be between 13,000 and 15,000 psi. ([[2014-ahmed|Ahmed, 2014, p. 59]])
- The optimization algorithm (DE) showed a stable objective function value starting at approximately Nfev = 3000 for the 12.25" section. ([[2014-ahmed|Ahmed, 2014, p. 84]])
- Bit coefficients for the 12.25" section were determined as a=0.002 and b=24.698, with c=0.00109. ([[2014-ahmed|Ahmed, 2014, p. 61]])
- In the 12.25" section, optimization reduced drilling time from 16.67 days to 13.3 days (a saving of 3.3 days) and reduced bit count from 4 to 3. ([[2014-ahmed|Ahmed, 2014, p. 93]])
- Optimization in the 12.25" section resulted in a total time reduction of approximately 27.5% compared to the actual case (from 14.5 days to 10.4 days). ([[2014-ahmed|Ahmed, 2014, p. 103]])
- The study found that higher ROP does not always lead to lower total time because high ROP can accelerate bit wear, necessitating more trips. ([[2014-ahmed|Ahmed, 2014, p. 121]])
- The optimal range for Wear Factor (Wf) at which bits should be pulled in the intermediate section was determined to be [0.25-0.4]. ([[2014-ahmed|Ahmed, 2014, p. 139]])
- In the main section, the optimal range of Wf values for bit replacement is [0.2-0.3]. ([[2014-ahmed|Ahmed, 2014, p. 139]])
- Increasing WOB to 42500 lbf in the main section was found to be more cost-effective than using maximum parameters, as it saved 1.5 days of rig time compared to the base case. ([[2014-ahmed|Ahmed, 2014, p. 140]])

## Limitations

Not stated in source

## Recommendations and Future Work

- Conduct rock mechanical tests on core samples from the field to ensure accuracy of rock strength data.
- Apply this work to deep wells.
- Apply this work on PDC models.
